narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 60% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in academic records, test scores, and backgrounds of past students, focusing on factors like grades, LSAT performance, and family circumstances that have historically shaped bar exam outcomes. The strongest influence pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st decile, which stands well below the 6th decile average among students who passed, signaling to the model a notable dip in later academic performance compared to successful peers. Next in importance, your LSAT score of 36 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since it's close to the 37.3 average for those who succeeded, showing your potential in standardized testing. Your Year 1 decile of 2nd also contributed toward failure, though less strongly, as it lags behind the typical 6th decile for passers, highlighting an early academic challenge. While your middle family income aligns with over a third of successful students and your choice of full-time study matches nearly all who passed, these didn't outweigh the key academic signals. Overall, your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, lower than the 3.24 average for bar passers, reinforced the concerns from your deciles, even if not the top factor here. Compared to typical accepted students, who often enter with stronger decile rankings across years and slightly higher LSATs, your profile appeared riskier for bar success in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your drive or background—many with similar starts have succeeded—but a data-driven call to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that positive pull, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted courses or tutoring, and perhaps gaining legal experience via internships to build a more competitive profile. Reapplying after addressing these areas could shift the prediction in your favor, as even small improvements in top factors like deciles have flipped decisions for others in your position. You're clearly committed, given your full-time plans, so channeling that into these steps will make a real difference.
